lines.text: SEGMENT 9 — Engraftment: the long wait
After the conditioning has wiped out the marrow and the new stem cells have been infused, the patient enters the most fragile period of the entire transplant process.
The infused cells are circulating in the blood, finding their way through the vasculature, looking for their niches.
The recipient has essentially no functional marrow.
Blood counts crash.
The platelets drop into single digits — normal is 150 to 400 thousand per microliter; transplant patients live at 10 or 20 for weeks.
The neutrophil count drops to zero.
The patient is, for a window of two to four weeks, profoundly immunocompromised and at high risk of bleeding.
Then, somewhere between day 10 and day 30 after infusion — depending on the source, the cell dose, the conditioning, and the recipient — engraftment happens.
The new stem cells have homed to the marrow niches, settled in, and begun to differentiate.
The first detectable sign is usually a rising neutrophil count.
We define neutrophil engraftment as the first of three consecutive days with an absolute neutrophil count above 500 per microliter — ANC > 500.
Platelet engraftment follows, usually a few days to weeks later, defined as platelets above 20,000 without transfusion support.
Red cell engraftment comes last, often weeks to months after, since red cells live long enough that the patient lives off transfusions during the early window.
Let me walk through what's actually happening at the cellular level, because the biology is beautiful.
When you infuse the donor cells into the vein, they don't just sit in the bloodstream.
They have somewhere to go.
CXCL12, the chemokine produced by the bone marrow stroma, leaks out into the bloodstream at low levels.
The infused HSCs express CXCR4, the CXCL12 receptor, on their surface.
As they circulate, they sense the CXCL12 gradient and follow it back toward its source — the bone marrow.

When the cells reach marrow capillaries, they bind to adhesion molecules on the endothelial cells — selectins, integrins like VLA-4 and LFA-1 — and squeeze through the vessel walls into the marrow space.
This is called transendothelial migration, and it's mechanistically similar to how immune cells extravasate at sites of inflammation.

Once in the marrow, the HSCs sample the local environment and find their niche — guided by additional signals from the stromal cells, the endothelium, and even from osteoblasts at the bone-marrow interface.
The whole process, from infusion to niche occupancy, takes hours to a few days.
The cells then begin to divide and differentiate.
The first daughter cells become committed progenitors, then mature blood cells, which take days to weeks to develop and exit into the bloodstream.
The kinetics depend on cell dose, conditioning intensity, and the source.
Peripheral blood stem cell grafts engraft faster than marrow, typically reaching ANC > 500 by day 12 to 14.
Marrow takes 18 to 21 days.
Cord blood is slowest, often 25 to 35 days, and graft failure is a real risk during the long window.
This engraftment gap — the two to four weeks during which the patient has no marrow function — is when most of the major early complications happen.
Bacterial infections from gut translocation (because the mucositis has stripped the gut barrier).
Fungal infections like aspergillosis.
Viral reactivations — CMV, EBV, HHV-6, BK virus.
Bleeding into critical organs.
Engraftment failure itself.
Hepatic veno-occlusive disease (now called sinusoidal obstruction syndrome), a particular liver complication of conditioning that can be fatal.
The clinical infrastructure built around HSCT is essentially built around managing this gap.
Patients are admitted to specialized transplant units with HEPA-filtered air, restricted visitor access, strict hand hygiene.
They get prophylactic antibiotics — typically a fluoroquinolone — antivirals like acyclovir, antifungals like voriconazole, PCP prophylaxis with TMP-SMX.
They get transfusions of platelets and red cells as needed.
They get daily blood counts, weekly viral PCRs, frequent fungal antigen testing.
Any fever triggers immediate broad-spectrum antibiotics.
It is an intensive, expensive, multidisciplinary undertaking, and a major transplant center may have dozens of patients in this fragile window at any given time.
When engraftment happens, it can be dramatic.
Patients who have been bedbound and pancytopenic for two weeks suddenly start producing white cells.
The fevers — often called "engraftment fevers" — flare and then resolve.
Blood counts climb day by day.
You can plot the daily ANC and watch the line go from 0 to 100 to 500 to 1000 to 5000 over the course of a week.
There is a clinical moment, well known to transplant nurses, when a patient says "I feel better today" for the first time since admission.
That moment usually coincides with neutrophil engraftment.
Once engraftment is established, the patient transitions out of the acute phase.
They are discharged from the hospital, often after 30 to 60 days of inpatient care.
They continue on antimicrobial prophylaxis, immunosuppression (for allogeneic), and close outpatient monitoring.
The immune system rebuilds over months to years — T cell reconstitution is particularly slow, often taking a year or more for full function.

During this period, the patient remains at elevated risk for opportunistic infections and, for allogeneic recipients, for graft-versus-host disease, which is the central topic of the second half of the lecture.
So engraftment is the bridge across the chasm that conditioning created.
New cells go in.
They home.
They occupy.
They divide.
The blood rebuilds.
The patient — if they survive the gap — gets a new blood and immune system.
It is one of the most concrete demonstrations in medicine that the body, given the right cells and the right conditions, can rebuild itself.
